title: Dog Bruising Around Genital Area: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Notice bruising on your dog's groin or genitals? This can be a sign of a serious medical emergency. Learn why immediate vet care is essential.
content_html:
TL;DR: Unexplained bruising on a dog's genitals or abdomen is a veterinary emergency that often indicates life-threatening blood clotting issues or poisoning. Seek immediate care if you notice purple or dark red splotches, especially if accompanied by lethargy or pale gums.

What does bruising in my dog's genital area look like and mean?

Bruising around the genitals or the inner thighs in dogs, often appearing as purple, dark red, or splotchy patches, is medically known as ecchymosis or petechiae. Unlike a simple scrape, this discoloration often suggests that blood is leaking from the vessels under the skin, which can indicate a systemic issue with how your dog's blood clots.

Is unexplained bruising on my dog’s genitals or belly a veterinary emergency?

  • Urgency Level: High. Unexplained bruising in the genital or abdominal area is a high-priority veterinary emergency.
  • It is often a primary sign of life-threatening conditions such as rodenticide (rat poison) ingestion, immune-mediated thrombocytopenia (IMTP), or severe internal trauma.
  • Contact an emergency veterinarian immediately if you see these marks.
  • Seek immediate help if your dog is also lethargic or has pale gums.

Why should I take a photo of my dog's bruising to help with triage?

  • Taking a clear, well-lit photo is incredibly helpful when calling a clinic or using a telehealth service.
  • A photo allows the veterinary team to visualize the extent and type of bruising, such as pin-point spots versus large coalescing bruises.
  • Visuals help the team determine the level of urgency and prepare the necessary diagnostic equipment before you arrive.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Bruising around the genital area in dogs may indicate several conditions, including trauma, vascular abnormalities, or penile masses, potentially leading to priapism (persistent erection without sexual stimulation). Injuries to the vulva and vagina can also result from trauma. Phimosis, an abnormally small preputial orifice, can be congenital or acquired due to neoplasia, edema, or fibrosis after trauma, inflammation, or infection. Priapism can be ischemic and constitute a medical emergency.

Chapter: Urology, Surgery, Reproduction

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1402)

faqs.question: Can I wait until morning to see my regular vet?
faqs.answer: No. Because bruising can be a sign of internal bleeding or a total failure of the clotting system, waiting even a few hours can lead to a crisis. Immediate evaluation is recommended.

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden bruising in dogs?
faqs.answer: Common causes include the ingestion of anticoagulant rat poisons, snake bites, blood clotting disorders (like Von Willebrand disease), or an autoimmune reaction where the body attacks its own platelets.

faqs.question: What will the vet do to treat the bruising?
faqs.answer: The vet will likely perform a physical exam and blood work, specifically a Complete Blood Count (CBC) and a coagulation profile, to determine why the blood is not clotting correctly.
